coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline using citric acid and lemon juice-derived carbon quantum dots incorporated TiO2 nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Nayoon
Tang, Cheng
Park, Yuri
Du, Aijun
Ayoko, Godwin A.
Hwang, Yuhoon
Chae, Soryong
coredata.dc:description: Tetracyclines (TC) are consistently found in aquatic ecosystems, contributing to the development and spread of antibiotic resistance genes and antibiotic-resistant bacteria. This poses adverse effects on aquatic organisms and humans, emphasizing the need for an effective approach to remove these contaminants. TiO2-based photocatalysts hold great potential for efficiently removing TC from water. This study aimed to synthesize TiO2 nanocomposites incorporated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from both artificial and natural sources. The nanocomposites were characterized using X-ray diffraction, electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and N2 adsorption–desorption test. The incorporation of CQDs into TiO2 resulted in the reduction of the band gap energy from 3.17 eV in pure TiO2 to 3.10 eV, broadening the light absorption range, and improving the charge carrier transfer efficiency. Continuously generated electrons led to the production of reactive oxygen species, predominantly •O2 –, contributing to TC degradation. Holes and •OH radicals also played a significant role in TC degradation. As a result, CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibited 91.5 % TC removal under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm) for 120 min at pH 8. The active sites of TC molecules were determined through density functional theory calculations, and the corresponding degradation pathways of TC were proposed using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry. Furthermore, this study attempted to synthesize CQDs using lemon juice, showcasing the potential of these eco-friendly photocatalysts for TC removal. The visible-light-driven degradation of TC by the natural-source-derived CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites demonstrated their applicability in water treatment to effectively address current environmental challenges.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracyclines (TC) are consistently found in aquatic ecosystems, contributing to the development and spread of antibiotic resistance genes and antibiotic-resistant bacteria. This poses adverse effects on aquatic organisms and humans, emphasizing the need for an effective approach to remove these contaminants. TiO2-based photocatalysts hold great potential for efficiently removing TC from water. This study aimed to synthesize TiO2 nanocomposites incorporated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from both artificial and natural sources. The nanocomposites were characterized using X-ray diffraction, electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and N2 adsorption–desorption test. The incorporation of CQDs into TiO2 resulted in the reduction of the band gap energy from 3.17 eV in pure TiO2 to 3.10 eV, broadening the light absorption range, and improving the charge carrier transfer efficiency. Continuously generated electrons led to the production of reactive oxygen species, predominantly •O2 –, contributing to TC degradation. Holes and •OH radicals also played a significant role in TC degradation. As a result, CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibited 91.5 % TC removal under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm) for 120 min at pH 8. The active sites of TC molecules were determined through density functional theory calculations, and the corresponding degradation pathways of TC were proposed using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry. Furthermore, this study attempted to synthesize CQDs using lemon juice, showcasing the potential of these eco-friendly photocatalysts for TC removal. The visible-light-driven degradation of TC by the natural-source-derived CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites demonstrated their applicability in water treatment to effectively address current environmental challenges.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various pharmaceutical compounds, several hundred types of antibiotics are consumed by humans and administered to animals on a large scale that have been detected persistently in aqueous environments [1–6]. For instant, annually 100,000–200,000 tons of antibiotics are used for human and agricultural purposes [7–9]. Among them, tetracycline (TC) is the second most extensively utilized antibiotic worldwide, serving as a therapeutic agent and growth promoter in both human medicine and the livestock industry [10,11].

According to the literature, TC is excreted at a high rate of 75–80 % due to incomplete metabolism and is subsequently discharged into wastewater through various routes [12,13]. TC has been detected in wastewater and drinking water sources at various concentrations, ranging from 17 to 1300 ng/L worldwide [14–16].

When these antibiotics are present in aquatic environment, organisms can absorb them and subsequently accumulate them in the food chain [12]. This bioaccumulation poses risks to both aquatic life and human health [17]. Another significant issue associated with the presence of antibiotics in the environment is the development of antibiotic-resistant bacteria (ARB). This occurrence can be attributed to the selection pressure imposed by the presence of antibiotics, leading to the proliferation of resistant strains [18]. In recent years, the incidence of ARB has been on the rise, largely due to the presence of discarded antibiotics in the environment. Therefore, many experts have emphasized the importance of identifying suitable methods to effectively remove antibiotics from wastewater sources [19].

Various treatment techniques such as adsorption, membrane filtration, and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been applied to treat wastewater containing pharmaceutical compounds [20–22]. Although various physical treatment technologies such as adsorption or membrane separation showed high removal efficiency of pharmaceutical compounds, these technologies necessitate a subsequent separation process to remove contaminants and materials, resulting in waste generation. Moreover, completely dissipating stable substances like pharmaceuticals after separation and disposal can be challenging, often requiring significant additional energy [23].

Photocatalytic processes are considered promising technologies for degrading persistent organic pollutants in water. Titanium(IV) oxide (TiO2) is one of the most widely used photocatalysts in many applications due to its advantages, such as strong oxidizing power, economic feasibility, commercial availability, non-toxicity, and biological and chemical inertness [24,25]. However, two drawbacks associated with TiO2-based photocatalysts are their large band gap energy and rapid electron-hole recombination, which restrict the use of TiO2 primarily to ultraviolet (UV) light [26].

Many researchers have attempted to overcome the aforementioned challenges related to TiO2 by doping it with metals and carbon materials, resulting in the modification of the surface and morphology of TiO2 [27–31]. Particularly, the construction of TiO2-based heterojunctions using carbon-based materials (e.g., carbon nanotubes [32], graphene [33], graphitic carbon nitride [34]) or, more recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), has emerged as an efficient approach to enhance the photocatalytic performance of emerging photocatalysts in pollutant removal under visible light. This approach enhances surface adsorption and facilitates the transport of charge carriers [35].

CQDs are zero-dimensional carbon-material-based semiconductor nanoparticles, possessing several advantages such as ease of synthesis, low toxicity, strong absorption wavelength band, and high electron-transfer efficiency [36,37]. Recently, there has been a growing utilization of CQDs in the formation of composites with TiO2. In this context, CQDs play a key role in enhancing charge-separation efficiencies by effectively trapping photoexcited electrons; as a result, the photocatalytic efficiency is significantly enhanced [38–41]. Despite several studies have showcased the efficacy of CQDs incorporated with TiO2 nanoparticles [40,42,43], there is still a need for a comprehensive investigation to understand and elucidate the mechanism responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/TiO2 composites.

Furthermore, the synthesis of CQDs has primarily relied on chemical precursors (such as citric acid and ascorbic acid), which incur significant reagent costs and generate unwanted chemicals and waste products, posing environmental concerns [44,45]. Recently, naturally derived CQDs have been developed as effective catalysts for environmental implications [46]. Utilizing natural sources like fruits and vegetables can reduce environmental impacts and offer economic feasibility. Indeed, citrus fruits, including lemon and grapefruit, are particularly abundant in carbon precursors suitable for synthesizing CQDs.

Notably, lemon juice contains approximately 48 g/L of citric acid, highlighting its potential for CQDs synthesis [47–49]. However, there is a critical knowledge gap regarding the direct application of lemon juice-derived-CQDs as a photocatalyst that absorbs visible light for TC degradation in water.

In this study, CQDs are incorporated into TiO2 (CQDs/TiO2) photocatalysts for the purpose of degradation TC in water. Initially, CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are prepared by combining CQDs derived from citric acid, a carbon precursor, and pure TiO2 at various ratios. The structural, morphological, spectroscopic, and optical properties of these CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are assessed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), N2 adsorption–desorption test, UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS), and photoluminescence (PL). Subsequently, the photocatalytic activities of these CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites in the oxidative degradation of TC are evaluated under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm).

Under the optimized operational conditions, the formation of byproducts and reactive oxygen radicals involved in TC degradation is assessed. Based on the evaluation, a mechanism for TC degradation by the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites is proposed, utilizing density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

Notably, CQDs derived from lemon juice, as a natural material source, are synthesized further and utilized to fabricate an environmentally friendly CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite with high photocatalytic efficiency for TC degradation. A comparative analysis of the photocatalytic performances of the two different types of CQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts under both laboratory and real water conditions is conducted.

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first comprehensive study on photocatalytic treatment of TC from real water samples using a green and stable CQDs-based catalyst. Therefore, the study provides an effective fabrication method of lemon-juice-derived-CQDs/TiO2 composites as naturally derived green photocatalyst with excellent performance in the area of pollutant degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: A complete list of chemical reagents used in this study is provided in Text S1 in the Supplementary Material. The synthesis procedure of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite samples is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Initially, CQDs were synthesized using a modified version of the method described by Zhao et al. (2018) [37]. In brief, 3.1521 g of citric acid was added to 30 mL of deionized (DI) water, and the mixture was stirred using a stirring plate for approximately 5 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon reactor and maintained at 200 °C for 6 h. Afterward, the solution was cooled to room temperature, and the resulting CQDs solution was refrigerated at 4 °C. Moreover, the aforementioned CQDs solutions in water were subjected to freeze-drying for further characterization.

For the synthesis of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, various amounts of CQDs solution was mixed with TiO2 nanoparticles and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The resulting mixtures were then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, the precipitate containing the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites was washed separately with pure ethanol and deionized (DI) water. The final products were dried in a vacuum oven at 70 ℃ for 12 h, followed by finely grinding them in an agate mortar. Finally, the samples were stored in a desiccator. The CQDs/TiO2 powder samples were obtained by combining the TiO2 with different quantities of CQDs, and it was named using the molar ratio of citric acid and TiO2 used in synthesis (CQD/TiO2-x, x = relative molar ratio of citric acid; 1, 2, 4, and 8).

To synthesize the natural-source-based CQDs (L-CQDs) and the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites decorated with the natural-source-based CQDs (L-CQDs/TiO2), commercially available lemon juice was used without undergoing any treatment. Lemon juice was mixed with DI water in a ratio of 2:1 and transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal synthesis of L-CQDs. Following the reaction, the resulting solution was filtered through a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane with a nominal pore size of 0.45 um (Seowon Mercury, Republic of Korea). The brownish-yellow solution containing the L-CQDs was washed twice with dichloromethane to remove organic substances. Subsequently, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate any remaining impurities.
The prepared L-CQDs solutions were then used for the synthesis of L-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites under the same conditions as described previously.

The synthesized CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, as well as TiO2, underwent characterization using XRD, SEM-EDS, TEM, FT-IR, BET surface analyzer, and XPS. In addition, PL and UV-DRS analyses were conducted on the prepared CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. Further information on the characterization methods can be found in Text S2.

Photocatalytic tests were conducted in the visible light region by using a Max-350 Xenon light source (Asahi Spectra, Japan) equipped with a UV cut-off filter (λ ≥ 400 nm). The nominal light intensity was measured as 106 W/m2. A TC solution with an initial concentration of 20 mg/L was prepared in DI water. 100 mL of this TC solution was poured into separate quartz vessels containing photocatalysts at various dosages (i.e., 50, 100, 200, and 300 mg/L).

Under continuous and gentle stirring, the system was allowed to reach adsorption equilibrium for 30 min under dark conditions before the photocatalytic evaluation commenced. The photocatalytic effect was evaluated over a 2-hour period under visible light irradiation.

The kinetics of the photocatalytic reaction were investigated under near-neutral pH conditions (i.e., pH = 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9) by controlling the pH using 0.5 M HCl and 0.5 M NaOH solutions. Sampling times for the kinetics calculation were set at 5, 15, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min. All samples were filtered through a 0.20 μm polyethersulfone (PES) filter (Seowon Mercury, Republic of Korea) and subsequently analyzed using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (detailed information provided in Section 2.5).

The catalyst materials used in this study were precipitated for a duration of 2 h, after which the supernatant was discarded. The resulting precipitate was washed twice with DI water and ethanol. Subsequently, the precipitate was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h and then employed in a reusability test. The reaction conditions for each of the three successive cycles were as follows: a catalyst dose of 200 mg/L, an initial TC concentration of 20 mg/L, and a 30-min adsorption equilibration period in the dark. This was followed by irradiation with visible light for 120 min.

The effectiveness of CQDs/TiO2 photocatalytic activity was further confirmed using real water samples obtained from the Paldang (PD) lake, a major water source in Seoul, South Korea, and wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) effluent collected from the Icheon WWTP (IC WWTP) that treats domestic wastewater and animal manure.

The collected water samples were transported to the laboratory and filtered through 0.45 μm PES filters within 24 h of collection, then stored at 4 °C for subsequent water quality analysis. The water quality data for the collected water samples are summarized in Table S1 , and details regarding the types, chemical structures, and concentrations of detected antibiotics in the PD Lake and IC WWTP effluent are provided in Table S2 .

TC degradation efficiency in the lake water sample was obtained by spiking TC into the PD lake water to achieve a concentration of approximately 2 μg/L of TC. Photocatalytic experiments were conducted with 200 mg/L CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, and the experimental procedure remained consistent. No additional pH adjustments or treatments were applied during the photocatalytic experiment.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic efficiencies of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites toward various antibiotics detected in the WWTP effluent were assessed. The IC WWTP effluent was directly used for the photocatalytic reaction without spiking TC solution or any additional treatment. Following the reaction, all samples were pre-treated and quantified using liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Details regarding the pre-treatment method and LC-MS/MS analysis conditions are outlined in Text S3.

Scavenging experiments were conducted to elucidate the reaction mechanism of TC photodegradation under visible light irradiation and to identify the species influencing the photocatalytic process. The test followed the same procedure as the photocatalytic experiment, and various scavengers, namely sodium azide (SA) (1O2 quencher), sodium oxalate (SO) (h+ quencher), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) (•OH quencher), and p-benzoquinone (BZQ) (•O2 – quencher), were employed to assess their impact.

The radicals involved in the degradation of TC using CQDs/TiO2-nanocomposites were identified using an electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (JES-FA200, JEOL, Japan). The ESR measurement were conducted at room temperature with the following parameters: a microwave frequency of 9.4 GHz, a magnetic field modulation frequency of 100 kHz, a microwave power of 1 mW, a modulation amplitude of 2 mT, a center field of 336 mT, a sweep width of 2.5 mT, and a sweep time of 30 s.

To identify the generated radicals, samples were collected from the reaction solution at different time intervals (i.e., 15 and 30 min), and the generated radicals were identified by adding spin adducts of 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) along with an appropriate quencher.

The TC samples collected before and after the photocatalytic reaction were analyzed using a high-performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC) (U-3000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with a mass spectrometer (MS) (ISQ-EC, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to quantify their TC concentrations. Chromatographic separation was achieved using a Thermo Fisher Accucore AQ C18 analytical column (Accucore AQ 4.6 × 150 mm, 2.6 μm) with a flow rate of 1 mL/min. The mobile phase consisted of a mixture of 0.1 % formic acid and acetonitrile (95:5, v/v) at a flow rate of 1 mL/min. During isocratic elution of the solvent mixture, 10 μL of the sample was injected through the column. The column temperature was maintained at 35 °C throughout the 9-min total runtime. For mass spectrometry, the following parameters were used: positive ion polarity using an electron impact-ion source (EIS), a retention time of 2.09 min, and a mass of 445 for TC.

The total organic carbon (TOC) content of both raw and treated TC solutions was determined using a TOC analyzer (Vario TOC Cube, Elementar LTD., Germany). The TOC removal rate of TC was calculated using the following equation:
(1)

where TOC0 and TOCt (mg/L) are the TOC concentrations at initial time and final time of the photocatalytic reaction, respectively. By calculating the TOC removal rate, the extent of TC degradation during the photocatalytic process can be evaluated.

An LC system (Thermo Vanquish; Thermo Scientific, USA) coupled with a tandem mass spectrometer (TSQ Quantis, Thermo Scientific, USA) was utilized to identify the degradation intermediates of TC following the photocatalytic reaction. More detailed information on the LC-MS analysis and methodology is described in Text S4 and
Table S3 .

DFT calculations were performed to investigate the optical properties of TiO2 with and without CQDs adsorption using the Vienna ab-initio simulation package (VASP) [50,51]. To improve computational efficiency, the projector augmented-wave (PAW) method was employed [52]. A vacuum layer of over 15 Å was set in the surface model of TiO2. For the TiO2 bulk, a reciprocal k-mesh of 11 × 11 × 3 Monkhorst-pack grid was used, while a 3 × 3 × 1 Gamma-centered mesh was employed for the surface model. The kinetic cut-off energy was set to 500 eV.

To obtain more accurate optical properties, the Heyd–Scuseria–Ernzerhof (HSE) functional was adopted [53]. In addition, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and Hirshfeld charge distribution of TC were determined using the Gaussian 16 package with the B3LYP exchange–correlation functional at the 6-31G* basic level [54–57]. These calculations provide insight into the electronic structure and charge distribution of TC, contributing to the understanding of its interactions with TiO2, CQDs, and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites.

The FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized CQDs was analyzed to identify the chemical functional groups present on their surface (Fig. 2
a). A broad peak at 2900–3500 cm−1 and a sharp band at 1430 cm−1 indicated the presence of O–H stretching and bending vibration, respectively. These vibrations were attributed to the hydroxyl group associated with adsorbed water [58]. A small peak at 2939 cm−1 was assigned to C–H stretching vibration. The strong band observed at 1713 cm−1 indicated the presence of C = O stretching vibration, which originated from carboxyl groups. Additionally, a peak at 1219 cm−1 was observed, which corresponded to the C-O stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group in the CQDs [59,60].

The particle size and lattice pattern of the synthesized CQDs were examined using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 b, the measured particle size of the CQDs was found to be in the range of 2.1–2.4 nm. This value is consistent with the particle sizes reported in previous studies that employed similar synthetic methods [61]. Furthermore, lattice fringes were observed in the TEM images, with a spacing of 0.21 nm. These lattice fringes corresponded to the (100) plane of the CQDs, confirming their crystalline structure [62]. The FTIR and TEM results provide supporting evidence for the successful synthesis of CQDs, as indicated by the presence of characteristic functional groups in the FT-IR spectrum and the observed particle size and lattice pattern in the TEM images.

To characterize the CQDs/TiO2 samples prepared with various CQDs:TiO2 ratios, several analyses were performed. XRD analysis was conducted to determine the crystal structure of both TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites (Fig. 2 c). In the XRD spectra, the diffraction peaks for pure TiO2 at angles of 25.3, 37.8, 48.0, 53.9, and 62.7° corresponded to (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), and (204) planes, respectively. These peaks correspond to the anatase form of TiO2 (JCPDS no.21–1272) [63].

The XRD spectra of all four CQDs/TiO2 samples exhibited typical single-phase anatase TiO2 patterns, similar to pure TiO2. There was no noticeable change in the XRD patterns of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites compared to pure TiO2. This indicates that the introduction of CQDs onto the TiO2 surface did not significantly alter the crystal structure of TiO2 [36].

Full FT-IR spectra of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites and TiO2 were recorded in the wavelength range of 450–4000 cm−1, as depicted in Figure S1 . Magnified spectral images focusing on the ranges of 1750–4000 cm−1, 1000–2250 cm−1, and 450–1000 cm−1 are presented separately in Fig. 2 d. These spectra provide detailed information about the chemical functional groups and molecular vibrations present in the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites and TiO2 samples.

The spectral images of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites revealed several characteristic peaks corresponding to specific vibrational modes as follows: the O–H stretching vibration appeared in the range of 2900–3500 cm−1. Moreover, the peaks in the region of 450–700 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O groups [59]. The peaks at 1403 cm−1 and 1629 cm−1 corresponded to the O–H bending vibration and Ti-OH stretching vibration originating from TiO2, respectively [60,64]. The bands at 1219 cm−1 and 1713 cm−1, specific to the spectra of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, were assigned to the C-O and C = O stretching vibrations, respectively, originating from the CQDs. These results confirmed the presence of abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups from the CQDs on the surface of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, along with the Ti-OH functional group from TiO2, thereby highlighting the existence of hydrogen bonding [65].

TEM analysis was conducted to investigate all the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. In all the samples, two different lattice fringes were observed simultaneously, as shown in Figure S2 . The lattice spacing values were nearly identical across all the samples. As an example, the TEM image and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CQDs/TiO2-4 sample are presented in Fig. 2 e, confirming the coexistence of lattices with spacings of 0.32 nm and 0.23 nm on the same layer. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.32 nm corresponded to the crystal structure (101) of TiO2 [36,66]. On the other hand, the 0.23 nm spacing coincided with the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (sp2 hybrid carbon), indicating the lattice structure of the CQDs [67]. This observation provides evidence of the presence of TiO2 and CQDs within the CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites.

SEM analysis was conducted on all the CQDs/TiO2 samples, and the resulting SEM images are shown in Figure S3 . These images visually confirmed the coexistence of TiO2 nanoparticles and smaller CQD particles on the surface of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. SEM images of the CQDs/TiO2-4 were captured at different magnifications, as illustrated in Fig. 2 f and g. These images revealed a uniform particle size and the presence of CQDs on the surface.

To further examine the elemental distribution, EDS mapping was performed. EDS mapping provide information about the carbon derived from the CQDs, titanium derived from TiO2, and oxygen on the surface of the synthesized CQDs/TiO2-4 (Fig. 2 h–k). The EDS mapping showed that all elements, particularly carbon, were evenly distributed over the material surface, indicating that the CQDs were well dispersed on the surface of the photocatalyst.

The carbon contents in the CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composites synthesized at various ratios were confirmed through element analysis and summarized in Table S4 . The results confirmed that as the ratio of CQDs in the complex increased, the content of organic elements such as C also increased. This phenomenon provides sufficient evidence that higher dosages of CQDs led to a higher amount of loading. The calculated content of CQDs in CQDs/TiO2-4 was 5.6 wt%.

The specific surface area and pore structure information of the prepared CQDs/TiO2 samples and TiO2 were studied by conducting N2 adsorption–desorption tests. As shown in Figure S4 , all the curves exhibited the Type IV isotherm, as classified by IUPAC, which is associated with capillary condensation within mesopores [68]. The measured BET surface areas, total pore volumes, and average pore sizes were 63.10 m2/g, 0.35 cm3/g, and 222.6 Å, respectively ( Table 1
). According to the results of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, their surface area and total pore volume decreased, but the average pore size increased relative to those of TiO2. This result can be explained by the loading of nano-sized CQDs on the TiO2 surface, which blocked the pores of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites [69].

Additionally, it indicates that the CQDs were loaded within the TiO2 mesopores [70]. Among the synthesized CQDs/TiO2 samples, the surface area and pore volume of CQDs/TiO2-4 were determined to be the largest at 59.5 m2/g and 0.33 cm3/g, respectively, while its pore size was the smallest at 225.0 Å. Given that a larger BET surface area can induce better contact with contaminants and provide more active sites, CQDs/TiO2-4 can be expected to exhibit higher photocatalytic efficiency compared to the other CQDs/TiO2 nanomaterials [71].

To investigate the elemental compositions and chemical interactions of the synthesized samples, additional XPS analyses were performed, and the survey spectra of all the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are presented in Figure S5 .

Six major peaks were detected and identified as Ti 2 s, O 1 s, Ti 2p, C 1 s, Ti 3 s, and Ti 3p. The corresponding major constituent elements in the XPS spectra of TiO2, CQDs, and CQDs/TiO2-4 are shown in Fig. 3
a, and the remaining spectra are described in Figure S6 . In Fig. 3 a, O1s peaks appeared in all the spectra, and Ti 2 s, Ti 2p, Ti 3 s, and Ti 3p peaks were observed in the spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-4. Additionally, C 1 s peaks were observed in the spectra of the CQDs and CQDs/TiO2-4. The raw data were analyzed through Gaussian fitting to clearly distinguish the XPS patterns.

In Fig. 3 b, 3c, and d, the results of high-resolution spectral analysis of the C 1 s, O 1 s, and Ti 2p peaks of CQDs/TiO2-4 are presented, and changes in the binding energies and intensities of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-4 are visually compared in terms of their O 1 s and Ti 2p peaks [72,73].

In the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CQDs/TiO2-4, four distinct peaks were observed, corresponding to the C–C/C = C (284.8 eV), C-O (286.3 eV), C = O (288.3 eV), and C-O-Ti (289.2 eV) bonds. The presence of the C-O-Ti bond proved the exist of the chemical bond between CQDs and TiO2, and also indicated the possibility of carbon existing in the TiO2 nanoparticle lattice, without replacing oxygen [74,75]. Moreover, the detected peak intensity was relatively low, suggesting that the C-O-Ti bond was not the major bond in CQDs/TiO2-4 [36].

The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of CQDs/TiO2-4 exhibited three peaks, assigned to the Ti-O bond (529.6 eV), C = O bond (530.1 eV), and O–H bond (531.5 eV). This result is consistent with the findings from the FT-IR analysis. In comparison, the O1s spectrum of TiO2 displayed only two peaks at 529.4 eV and 531.2 eV, corresponding to the Ti-O bond and O–H bond, respectively.

The relative percentages of the C = O and O–H bond areas in all samples are summarized in Table S5 . The C = O bond represented a carboxyl functional group derived from the CQDs, and the peak areas of the C = O bond were observed to be 15.5, 27.9, 18.0, and 14.9 % for the CQDs/TiO2-8, CQDs/TiO2-4, CQDs/TiO2-2, and CQDs/TiO2-1 samples, respectively [36].

The O–H bond, associated with the presence of a hydroxyl group, was measured as 13.8 % for TiO2. Higher values of 13.6, 15.6, 18.4, and 18.7 % were obtained for the CQDs/TiO2 nanomaterials, indicating an increased presence of CQDs on their surfaces.

All these results suggest that an increased proportion of hydrophilic functional groups in the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, indicating effective decoration of CQDs on the TiO2 surface. Interestingly, the ratios of these functional groups exhibited a nonlinear increase with increasing CQDs dosage.

This behavior could be attributed to the loading of CQD nanoparticles not only on the surface but also within the TiO2 pores, which is consistent with the BET analysis result. Regarding the ratio of the Ti-O bond derived from TiO2, the TiO2 sample exhibited a high ratio of 86.2 %, which decreased to 53.7 % for the CQDs-loaded CQDs/TiO2 samples, with the exception of CQDs/TiO2-8.

The high-resolution Ti 2p spectra of CQDs/TiO2-4 and TiO2 exhibited four peaks with similar shapes, although there were slight shifts in the binding energies. In the spectrum of CQDs/TiO2-4, Ti was resolved into Ti3+ 2p3/2 (456.9 eV), Ti4+ 2p3/2 (458.6 eV), Ti3+ 2p1/2 (460.3 eV), and Ti4+ 2p1/2 (464.3 eV). The same peaks were detected in TiO2 with values of 456.7, 458.4, 460.5, and 464.1 eV, respectively.

It is noteworthy that Ti4+ was the predominant state of Ti in both materials. Furthermore, the composition ratio of Ti3+ among the states of Ti in TiO2 was 9.9 %, while in the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites incorporating CQDs, it ranged from 10.4 % to 12.7 %. The appearance and increased peak of Ti3+ indicate that CQDs changed the chemical bond environment around Ti, and this observation can be attributed to two causes. First, introducing carbon may reduce Ti4+ in TiO2 to Ti3+, supporting the formation of Ti2O3 [69,76]. Changes in the TiO2 state induced by self-doping with Ti3+ and the generation of oxygen vacancies (Vo) play crucial roles in enhancing the visible light absorption and photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 [77,78,79]. Also, carbon atoms may enter the lattice of TiO2 to form Ti-C bonds, increasing the electron density around Ti atoms and reducing the binding energy. Therefore, the changes in Ti ions and the shifting of the Ti 2p peaks in the CQDs/TiO2 samples suggest an improved surface state of TiO2 due to the introduction of CQDs.

All characterization results presented thus far confirm the successful synthesis of CQDs and their effective dispersion on the surface and within the interiors of TiO2. Furthermore, these results validate the presence of electrostatic interactions and chemical bonding between hydrophilic functional groups, such as the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, in the CQDs/TiO2 samples.

Among the synthesized CQDs/TiO2 samples, CQDs/TiO2-4 exhibited the highest intensity (Fig. 4a), indicating its improved light adsorption capacity, even in the visible light region. Additionally, the band gap energies of the CQDs/TiO2 samples were determined from the Tauc plot (Fig. 4
b). Compared to the band gap energy of TiO2, which was measured as 3.21 eV in this study and is consistent with the values reported in other studies [80,81], the band gap energies of the CQD/TiO2 samples decreased to 3.10 eV for CQDs/TiO2-4 and CQDs/TiO2-8 samples, and to 3.17 eV for the other CQDs/TiO2 samples. This indicates that the introduction of CQDs adjusted the band gap of the CQDs/TiO2 samples, potentially enhancing their photocatalytic performance in the visible light region.

The PL spectra of the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were measured under an excitation wavelength of 325 nm to investigate various aspects, such as charge carrier trapping, migration and transfer, defects, surface oxygen vacancies, and the lifetime of hole-electron pairs, following the principle that intensity and capacity are positively correlated [82].

An increase in PL intensity indicates fast recombination of hole-electron pairs, which implies a decrease in photocatalytic activity. As shown in Figure S7 , all the CQDs/TiO2 samples exhibited a broad PL emission peak in the 400–500 nm range, which was attributed to radiative recombination through self-trapping excitation [83]. The PL intensities of the CQDs/TiO2 samples decreased with the incorporation of more CQDs into TiO2 in the following order: CQDs/TiO2-1 > CQDs/TiO2-2 > CQDs/TiO2-4 > CQDs/TiO2-8. This result indicates that the effective promotion of photo-generated electron-hole pair separation can be expected upon CQDs loading. Therefore, CQDs/TiO2-8 or even CQDs/TiO2-4 may exhibit higher efficiency in promoting charge-carrier separation and enhancing photocatalytic activity compared to the other CQDs/TiO2 samples.

The geometrically optimized structure of the CQDs/TiO2-4 interface was simulated using DFT calculations to elucidate the changes in the optical properties of CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites resulting from the incorporation of CQDs onto TiO2. Herein, the CQD/TiO2-4 surface consists of five oxygen layers [along (001) facet of anatase TiO2] with the bottom two layers fixed, while the CQDs are built by a pyrene structure with four fused benzene rings, which is commonly adopted in the pioneering research [84]. As shown in Fig. 4 c, the CQDs exhibited van der Waals interactions with the O terminal of the TiO2 (001) facet, and the interlayer distance between the CQDs in CQDs/TiO2-4 was calculated to be 2.46 Å, consistent with previous reports on the doping of TiO2 surfaces with graphene [85,86].

According to literature, the interface between the doped CQDs and TiO2 is primarily governed by weak dispersion forces [85]. In this case, the CQDs exhibited van der Waals interactions with the O terminal of the TiO2 (001) facet, and the interlayer distance between the CQDs in CQDs/TiO2-4 was calculated to be 2.46 Å. This finding is consistent with previous reports on the doping of TiO2 surfaces with graphene [86].

Furthermore, the light absorbance of TiO2 with and without CQDs modification was measured. As shown in Fig. 4 d, pure TiO2 exhibited low light absorption in the region below 400 nm due to its inherent bandgap (i.e., 3.21 eV for TiO2 in this study). However, in the spectrum of CQDs/TiO2-4, an adsorption peak was observed around 600 nm even after CQDs decoration. This indicated that a significant improvement in the light absorbance of TiO2 following CQDs modification. Consequently, the introduction of CQDs into TiO2 complexes broadened the spectral range for light absorption, enhanced the light absorption capacity, and suppressed electron-hole recombination, thereby promoting visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of TC using CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites
TC photodegradation under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic efficiencies of the characterized CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were evaluated for the removal of TC as the target contaminant under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5
a, negligible TC removal was observed in the absence of the photocatalyst. In addition, the adsorption equilibrium was achieved for the 30 min in the dark condition, and the adsorption efficiencies of all CQDs/TiO2 composites were measured in low level, 4.82 % for CQDs/TiO2-1, 3.45 % for CQDs/TiO2-2, 6.49 % for CQDs/TiO2-4, and 5.27 % for CQDs/TiO2-8.

Theoretically, TiO2 is not activated in the visible light range. However, as reported in a previous study, the commercially available TiO2 used in this study seemed to exhibit visible light activation for TC removal [87]. Additionally, the Xenon lamp utilized in the batch experiment provided high intensity in the 420–500 nm range, which corresponds to the high-energy visible light spectrum, and may have contributed to the activation of TiO2.

Regarding the efficiency of the four different CQDs/TiO2 compounds, CQDs/TiO2-4 exhibited the highest efficiency with a photo-removal rate of 62.3 %, followed by CQDs/TiO2-8 (54.7 %), CQDs/TiO2-2 (49.1 %), and CQDs/TiO2-1 (48.3 %). This outcome indicates that the introduction of CQDs onto TiO2 enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency. However, excessive loading of CQDs on TiO2, as seen in CQDs/TiO2-8, somewhat inhibited the photocatalytic performance, due to the shielding effect, which reduced the number of active sites where reactive species could be generated [36,88].

Considering these results, CQDs/TiO2-4 was selected as the most efficient photocatalyst for the subsequent optimization tests. The optimal dosage test was performed using CQDs/TiO2-4. As depicted in Fig. 5 b, the TC removal rate increased with an increase in the photocatalyst dose during the 2-h reaction. However, the TC degradation did not continue to increase beyond a certain threshold, even with higher doses (i.e., 200 mg/L or more). Consequently, TC removal rates of 68.8 % and 70.6 % were achieved by adding 200 mg/L and 300 mg/L of the photocatalysts, respectively. Therefore, an optimal dose of 200 mg/L of CQDs/TiO2-4 photocatalyst was chosen, considering both economic feasibility and efficiency.

A kinetics experiment was conducted on CQDs/TiO2-4 under various near-neutral pH conditions (i.e., pH 5–9). The kinetics data obtained at pH 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 were fitted to a pseudo first-order kinetic model (Fig. 5 c).

Overall, the reaction rate increased as the pH increased. The kinetic constants (K
obs ) of CQDs/TiO2-4 were calculated as 0.00801, 0.01156, 0.01482, 0.01949, and 0.02461 min−1 at pH 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9, respectively. Particularly, the reaction rate significantly improved under alkaline conditions, which can be attributed to the enhanced generation of hydroxyl (•OH). This phenomenon can also be explained by the production of •OH not only from water molecules but also directly from the abundant dissolved OH ions under alkaline conditions, as indicated in Equations
(2) , (3)
[89]:
(2)

+
+
H 2

H

+ ∙ O H
(3)

→ ∙ O H

Moreover, the unstable ionic structure of TC molecules in alkaline conditions can lead to faster destruction [90]. As a result, the highest effectiveness of the photocatalyst was observed at pH 9. However, considering the need to closely mimic the real operational conditions of wastewater treatment plants and achieve effective photodegradation of TC, pH 8 (which is a near-neutral alkaline condition) was selected as the optimal condition. Based on all the findings, we determined the optimal condition to be the application of 200 mg/L of CQDs/TiO2-4 at pH 8, and this condition was subsequently applied in all the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of predominant radicals involved in the TC photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
Scavenging activity tests were conducted to investigate the roles of various radicals using scavengers, namely p-benzoquinone (BZQ), isopropanol (IPA), sodium azide (SA), and sodium oxalate (SO), during TC degradation under visible light at pH 8 (Fig. 6
a).

The addition of BZQ significantly decreased the degradation efficiency of TC by 60.9 %. Similarly, the addition of SO reduced, the degradation efficiency of TC by 18.2 %. In contrast, the addition of IPA and SA resulted in a slight increase in the degradation efficiencies of TC by 1.3 % and 4.8 %, respectively, indicating negligible interfering effects. These results suggest that superoxide radicals (•O2 –) play a dominant role in the photocatalytic degradation of TC, followed by holes (h+), and subsequently, all other radicals (i.e., •OH and 1O2).

ESR analysis was performed to observe radical production. A signal peak representing hydroxyl radicals appeared as a 1:2:2:1 signal, as illustrated in Fig. 6 b. A clear •OH signal was detected 15 min after the photocatalyst was irradiated with visible light, but the signal intensity decreased starting from minute 30.

Continuously, 1:1:1 triplet signals of electrons (e-) were detected when the photocatalyst was irradiated with visible light, as shown in Fig. 6 c. The intensity of this strong and clear signal was maintained even after 30 min from the start of the reaction, suggesting the continuous formation of electrons. This phenomenon was attributed to the electron transport enhancement effect of the CQDs, which is expected to contribute to the improvement of TC photocatalysis. In addition, the continuous formation of electrons supports the adequate contribution of •O2 –, as described by the equation (e- + O2 → •O2 –), which explains the generation of •O2 – through the reduction of oxygen (O2).

To evaluate the reusability and economic feasibility of CQDs/TiO2-4, a recyclability test was conducted (Fig. 7
a). Although the overall efficiency seemed to decrease slightly in three consecutive experiments, when compared only with photocatalytic efficiency, a similarly high removal rate was maintained in all three cycles: 68.8 % in the first, 79.3 % in the second, and 72.8 % in the third. Additionally, when we compared the kinetic constants (Kobs) during the photocatalytic reaction, they were 0.1679 min−1 for the first, 0.2036 min−1 for the second, and 0.1589 min−1 for the third, the performance of recycled CQDs/TiO2 remained at similar levels. Overall removal efficiency seems to have decreased due to the decrease of the adsorption capacity from 13.6 % in the beginning to 6.8 % and 5.6 %, but it was confirmed that there was no effect on catalyst activity, demonstrating its stability and reusability.

TEM images and XPS spectra of fresh and used CQDs/TiO2-4 were compared to investigate any material deformation. As shown in Fig. 7 b, TEM images of the reused CQDs/TiO2-4 revealed different lattice fringes of 0.33 nm and 0.23 nm for TiO2 and CQDs, respectively. This is consistent with the results obtained for CQDs/TiO2-4 before the reaction (Fig. 2 e).

A comparison of the XPS analysis results of CQDs/TiO2-4 before and after its use is presented in Table S6 . The analysis of the high-resolution O1s spectra of the photocatalysts provided information on the binding energies and relative percentages of the C = O, O–H, and Ti-O bonds. The results showed a decrease in the ratio of the C = O bond originating from the CQDs and an increase in the ration of the Ti-O bond originating from TiO2, indicating that a few CQD particles on the surface of CQDs/TiO2-4 were lost. However, the Ti ion state remained almost constant, and there was no significant change in the binding energy value, suggesting that CQDs/TiO2-4 is chemically stable over the three reaction cycles. These results collectively suggest that CQDs/TiO2-4 can maintain its efficiency stably over multiple reuse cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathways of TC and photocatalytic degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the findings presented in this study, photocatalytic degradation of TC in water was conducted using the CQDs/TiO2-4 nanocomposite as the photocatalyst. The incorporation of CQDs led to a band gap of 3.10 eV in CQDs/TiO2-4, allowing it to be excited by visible light and generate electron-hole pairs.

The presence of CQDs created a new conduction band state, facilitating efficient charge transfer at the interfaces and enhancing the separation of the electron-hole pairs, thereby increasing the lifetime of CQDs/TiO2-4. Notably, electrons were continuously present during visible light irradiation and participated in the reduction of O2, converting it into singlet oxygen (1O2) or superoxide radicals (•O2 –). The holes (h+) themselves or the photo-generated holes may have contributed to the photocatalytic reaction by producing •OH radicals. Among the reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in TC decomposition, •O2 – played a major role in this study.

To gain insights into the TC degradation pathway by the CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite under visible light irradiation, LC-MS/MS analysis was conducted. Additionally, DFT-based chemical computations were performed to predict the active sites in TC based on the reactive radicals generated during the photocatalytic process. First-principle calculations were used to evaluate the molecular orbital and Fukui function of TC, providing information about its capability to attract and release electrons.

Fig. 8
a illustrates the distribution of the HOMO in TC, which was predominantly contributed by the salicylamide group. This indicates that the salicylamide group is the preferred reaction site for reaction with the electrophilic radicals (h+). On the other hand, the LUMO was located in different regions of TC, highlighting various nucleophilic sites.

To assess the reactivity of TC towards electrophilic and nucleophilic radicals, Fukui's index (f) was calculated based on the Hirshfeld charge distribution, as illustrated in Figure S8
a. The atomic indices are marked in Figure S8
b. The nucleophilic index [f + = q(N)-q(N + 1)] and electrophilic index [f -- = q(N-1)-q(N)] represent the susceptibility of TC to attack by nucleophilic and electrophilic radicals, respectively (Note: N represents the number of electrons, indicate the ability to be attacked by electrophilic and nucleophilic radicals, respectively). Sites C1-C5, C15, C17, O1, O7, and O8 exhibited relatively higher f+ values, indicating their affinity for nucleophilic radicals. Conversely, sites C10–C13, C15, O3–O5, and O7 had relatively higher f -- values, suggesting their suitability for attack by electrophilic radicals. This result is in good agreement with the above analysis on HOMO and LUMO orbitals. Based on the obtained experimental and DFT calculated model, a plausible mechanism of TC degradation using CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites is proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 8 b.

Based on the Fukui index analysis and the identification of TC intermediates through LC-MS/MS analysis, three primary decomposition pathways for TC were proposed, as shown in Fig. 9 . The corresponding TC degradation products, including their mass-to-charge ratios (m/z), retention times, and MS spectra, are summarized in Table S7
and
Figure S9 . These findings provide important information regarding the transformation products formed during the photocatalytic degradation of TC using CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites.

First possible pathway involves the attack by the acylamino group at the C11 site of TC molecules (as indicated in Figure S8
b). This attack leads to the formation of TP1 with an m/z = 427.14963 through a dehydration reaction. TP1 is then degraded into TP2 (m/z = 410.12302) through deamination. TP2 undergoes oxidation, resulting in the formation of TP3 (m/z = 365.06415) with the loss of the N-dimethyl group (or N-dimethylation) and dehydrogenation. This oxidation process is associated with the attack of h+ [91,92]. Then, TP3 can be further attacked by long-life ROS, leading to the formation of TP4 with an m/z = 241.08583. This transformation occurs through the cleavage of the double bonds of TP5 (m/z = 163.03860).

Another pathway is that TC molecules (m/z = 445.16) undergo an oxidation reaction, leading to the formation of TP6 (m/z = 459.13962). TP6 is then further transformed into TP7 (m/z = 414.11874) through a ring-opening reaction accomplished by the loss of the N-dimethyl group and hydroxyl group, which is facilitated by h+ and •O2 – radicals. It should be noted that the degradation of TC involves processes such as deamination, hydroxylation, dehydroxylation, and N-dimethylation mediated by ROS (such as h+, •O2 –, and •OH).

Additionally, pathway III could be involved by the attack on the aromatic ring of the tetracycline molecules, which have a relatively high electron density. This attack by •OH radicals leads to hydroxylation of the aromatic ring, resulting in the formation of two isomers of TP8. These isomers are observed as intermediate products during TC degradation in the previous study, with TP8a having a m/z = 461.15567 and TP8b having an m/Z of 461.15521 [93]. Subsequently, the attack by ROS can cause deamination of TP8a, leading to the formation of an intermediate TP10 with m/z = 400.13867. This deamination process involves the loss of two molecular hydrogens through a dehydrogenation reaction and oxidation reaction. Additionally, TP8b can be further transformed into TP10 with m/z = 442.11285.

The TC transformation products obtained in this study can undergo further decomposed into smaller molecules or may even be mineralized into CO2 and H2O. This is supported by the observation of 42 % TOC removal efficiency achieved using CQDs/TiO2-4 after a 120 min photocatalytic reaction, suggesting that complete mineralization of TC could potentially be achieved with a longer reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of lemon juice derived-CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites toward TC degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
Finally, this study assessed the photocatalytic efficiency of both CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites synthesized from the commercially available lemon juice for the removal of TC from aqueous solutions. Initially, the chemical functional groups of the CQDs obtained from lemon juice (referred to as L-CQDs) were compared with the CQDs synthesized using citric acid (Fig. 10
a). As a result, the comparison revealed identical trends, with only minor variations, and highlighted the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the L-CQDs. Subsequently, the particle size and lattice fringes of the L-CQDs were analyzed using TEM analysis (Fig. 10 b). The observed particle sizes of the L-CQDs ranged from 3.2 to 3.9 nm, with a corresponding d-spacing of 0.23 nm, which closely resembled the values obtained for the CQDs synthesized from citric acid in this study. These findings confirmed the successful synthesis of L-CQDs from commercially available lemon juice.

In addition, the crystal structural properties of L-CQDs/TiO2, synthesized with the same ratio as that of CQDs/TiO2-4, were compared with CQDs/TiO2-4 in Fig. 10 c and d. The XRD analysis confirmed that L-CQDs/TiO2 closely matched the pattern of anatase TiO2, consistent with all the CQDs/TiO2 composites. In the TEM image of L-CQDs/TiO2, simultaneous detection of lattice spacing values of 0.32 nm and 0.23 nm provided evidence of well-conjugated TiO2 and CQDs nanoparticles in the nanocomposite. These characterization results collectively confirm the successful synthesis of L-CQDs and L-CQDs/TiO2 composites with lemon, exhibiting a comparable structure to CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 synthesized with citric acid.

The photocatalytic performance of lemon juice-based L-CQDs/TiO2 was evaluated, and the results are presented in Fig. 11 . Initially, a batch test for TC removal was conducted under the predetermined optical conditions (Fig. 11 a). The results indicated similar photocatalytic degradation trends for both photocatalysts, except that the TC removal rate of L-CQDs/TiO2 was slightly higher at 94.8 %. This finding demonstrates the excellent ability of L-CQDs/TiO2 to remove TC under visible light irradiation.

However, all experiments were performed under conditions using DI water, and the applied concentration of TC (i.e., 20 mg/L) exceeded the typical concentration found in real water systems. The performance of photocatalysts in laboratory settings may differ from their performance in natural water due to various factors such as the presence of coexisting ions, inorganic and organic matter, and particles. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2-4 and L-CQDs/TiO2 were evaluated for TC removal in natural water matrices, including lake water.

Before treatment, the PD Lake water samples showed no detectable TC. Considering the average TC concentrations reported in the mainstream of the Han River in South Korea [15], the initial TC concentration was estimated to be approximately 2 μg/L (as shown in Fig. 11 b). After subjecting the CQDs/TiO2-4 and L-CQDs/TiO2 to photocatalytic reactions for 2 h, TC was detected at concentrations of 55.70 ng/L and 10.78 ng/L, respectively, indicating a substantial reduction in TC levels. The achieved removal efficiencies of 97.5 % and 99.5 % by CQDs/TiO2-4 and L-CQDs/TiO2, respectively, underscore their exceptional catalytic properties in degrading target contaminants in real water systems.

An additional photocatalytic degradation test was conducted using the IC WWTP effluent to assess the removal of various antibiotics detected in the effluent. As shown in Fig. 11 c, sulfadiazine, sulfamethazine, sulfamethoxazole, trimethoprim, doxycycline, lincomycin, tiamulin, and clopidol were detected in the IC WWTP effluent within the range of 1.2 to 146.5 ng/L. After undergoing photocatalytic degradation with CQDs/TiO2-4 and L-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, concentrations of almost all antibiotics decreased. Notably, doxycycline showed the highest efficiency, being completely removed by both CQDs/TiO2-4 and L-CQDs/TiO2, followed by sulfamethazine with an average removal rate of 61.0 %, and tiamulin with an average removal rate of 47.0 %. In contrast, clopidol and sulfadiazine showed lower removal efficiencies, averaging about 6.9 %. Although the removal efficiencies varied for each antibiotic depending on the photocatalyst used, all detected antibiotics exhibited changes in concentration after the photocatalytic reaction. Thus, the results underscore the potential of natural-source-derived CQDs/TiO2 for effective treatment of real water under visible light.

The environmental application of previously reported CQDs/TiO2 composites derived from not only citric acid but also various carbon sources, including glucose, carbonized starch, ascorbic acid, graphite rods are summarized in Table S8. Several studies additionally used N-containing precursors such as monoethanolamide, 4, 7, 10-trioxa-1, and 13-tridecanediamine to synthesize CQDs. Similar to our study, there have also been several studies that synthesized CQDs using a single carbon-based precursor, but their applications were primarily focused on the removal of organic dyes and selected pharmaceuticals such as cefradine, propranolol and levofloxacin [40,43,73,94–97]. Also, research on CQDs and oxygen-rich titanium dioxide nanosheet composites applied to photocatalytic degradation of TC has been reported recently [39]. Distinctively, our study showed high TC removal efficiency under visible light using the CQDs/TiO2 synthesized using a facile synthesis procedure and minimal materials. Additionally, this study uniquely used lemon juice as a precursor for preparing CQDs and tested their efficacy for TC degradation, demonstrating the first such report. This kind of research is rather noteworthy in terms of economics and practicality.

Direct comparison of degradation rates and efficiencies is challenging due to variations in test conditions across the literature. However, the demonstrated 94.8 % removal efficiency of L-CQDs/TiO2 highlights the potential of natural-source-derived CQDs and CQDs-incorporated TiO2 nanocomposites as effective photocatalysts for degrading emerging contaminants in water. Additionally, the CQDs/TiO2 composites showed promise for the photocatalytic removal of TC and various other antibiotics found in natural water sources. Further investigation is warranted to comprehensively understand the photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of TC and other emerging contaminants when utilizing L-CQDs and L-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites for their removal.

In this study, both CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite were synthesized using simple methods employing artificial (i.e., citric acid) and natural (i.e., lemon juice) carbon precursors. Physicochemical properties of the synthesized materials were analyzed using XRD, TEM, and SEM, revealing the successful incorporation of anatase TiO2 and the CQDs. FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the material surface, while XPS analysis suggested the formation of CQDs/TiO2 through hydrogen bonding and chemical interactions.

Among the various synthesized ratios, CQDs/TiO2-4 exhibited the highest specific surface area and visible light absorption ability. The introduction of CQDs reduced the band gap energy of TiO2 from 3.21 eV to 3.10 eV, consequently enhancing its photocatalytic performance under visible light. Under optimal conditions (i.e., pH 8 and catalyst dosage of 200 mg/L), CQDs/TiO2-4 achieved a remarkable TC removal efficiency of 91.5 % (initial TC concentration = 20 mg/L) under visible light irradiation for 2 h, The material demonstrated stability over three consecutive cycles.

Overall, the modification of TiO2 with the synthesized CQDs effectively separated charge carriers, leading to the continuous formation of active species with strong redox potentials. Predominantly, active species involved in TC decomposition were identified as •O2 –, followed by h+ and •OH.

DFT calculations predicted the active sites of TC molecules, initially damaged by reactive oxygen radicals. Consequently, several TC intermediates were formed, with subsequent TOC analysis indicating further mineralization into CO2 and H2O.

Moreover, the incorporation of lemon juice derived-CQDs into TiO2 (i.e., L-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites) achieved a remarkable photocatalytic TC degradation efficiency of 94.8 %. Furthermore, the excellent removal capacity of L-CQDs and CQDs-based photocatalysts under real water conditions was demonstrated, not only for TC but also for various antibiotics. This study highlights a promising approach for the synthesis of sustainable visible-light-driven photocatalysts using natural sources for water treatment applications.
